relationship_to_restored_text: Interpretive contemplative layer aligned by verse. This does not replace the source-audited restored translation.

verses.mystical_translation: The earth spoke with one language and one set of words, unified before its unity became ambition.
Humanity settled in Shinar, gathering itself into one place.
They turned technology toward construction, making brick and mortar for a shared project.
They sought to build a city and tower into the heavens, to make a name for themselves and avoid being scattered.
The Eternal came down to see the tower, and the grand human project was smaller than it imagined.
The Divine saw one people with one language, unified in power and unchecked intention.
The Eternal confused their language, breaking the spell of centralized ambition.
They were scattered over the earth, and the city-building ceased.
The place was called Babel, because the language was confused there and humanity was scattered from there.
The story narrowed from the nations to Shem’s line, preparing the way toward Abram.
Shem’s life continued, and the line carried forward.
Arpachshad fathered Shelah.
Arpachshad’s years widened through sons and daughters.
Shelah fathered Eber, whose name would echo into Hebrew identity.
Shelah’s life continued through generations.
Eber fathered Peleg, child of division.
Eber’s line continued through sons and daughters.
Peleg fathered Reu.
Peleg’s life continued beyond the named son.
Reu fathered Serug.
Reu’s line continued through sons and daughters.
Serug fathered Nahor.
Serug’s life continued through the line.
Nahor fathered Terah.
Nahor’s life continued, carrying the line toward Abram.
Terah fathered Abram, Nahor, and Haran, and the next great movement began to appear.
The generations of Terah opened the family of Abram, Lot, and the journey ahead.
Haran died in the land of his birth, leaving Lot within the family story of Abram.
Abram and Nahor took women, and Sarai entered the story.
Sarai was barren, and the impossible place of promise was named before the promise arrived.
Terah’s household left Ur toward Canaan, but settled in Haran before the call would draw Abram onward.
Terah’s days ended in Haran, and the stage was set for Abram to continue the journey.
